metadata.category: Arrays | Mathematics | Number Theory | Fast Fourier Transform

content.problemStatement:
You're given an array of integers AAA and a permutation PPP, each of size NNN. Recall that a permutation of length NNN is an array where every element from 111 to NNN occurs exactly once.

The following operation is performed on the array KKK times:


For each index iii (1≤i≤N1 \le i \le N1≤i≤N), add APiA_{P_i}APi​​ to AiA_iAi​ **simultaneously**.
For example, if A=[2,6,1,7]A = [2, 6, 1, 7]A=[2,6,1,7] and P=[3,1,4,2]P = [3, 1, 4, 2]P=[3,1,4,2], after one operation A=[2+A3,6+A1,1+A4,7+A2]=[3,8,8,13]A = [2 + A_3, 6 + A_1, 1 + A_4, 7 + A_2] = [3, 8, 8, 13]A=[2+A3​,6+A1​,1+A4​,7+A2​]=[3,8,8,13].

Find the values of AAA modulo 998244353998244353998244353, after performing the above operation KKK times.


Input Format

The first line contains two space separated integers NNN and KKK.
The second line contains NNN space separated integers A1,A2,…ANA_1, A_2, \dots A_NA1​,A2​,…AN​, the elements of the array AAA.
The third line contains NNN space separated integers P1,P2,…PNP_1, P_2, \dots P_NP1​,P2​,…PN​, the elements of the permutation PPP.


Output Format
Print NNN space separated integers, the elements of the array AAA modulo 998244353998244353998244353 after performing the above operation KKK times.
constraints: P is a permutation of length N
content.inputFormat: The first line contains two integers N and K (1 ≤ N, K ≤ 2·10^5). The second line contains N integers A1 … AN (0 ≤ Ai < 998244353). The third line contains N integers P1 … PN – a permutation of {1,…,N}. All indices are 1‑based.
content.outputFormat: Print N integers – the array A after performing the described operation K times, each value taken modulo 998244353.

solution.approach: The operation can be written as A' = (I + M)·A where M is the permutation matrix of P. Repeating K times gives A^{(K)} = (I+M)^K·A. For a cycle of length L the matrix (I+M) is a circulant matrix whose K‑th power equals (I+S)^K where S is the cyclic shift on the cycle. By the binomial theorem (I+S)^K = Σ_{t=0}^{K} C(K,t)·S^{t}. Because S^{L}=I, only the remainder t mod L matters. Define coeff[r] = Σ_{0≤t≤K, t≡r (mod L)} C(K,t). Then for a cycle c[0…L‑1] the final value at position j is Σ_{r=0}^{L‑1} coeff[r]·A_{c[(j+r) mod L]}. This is a circular convolution of the original values on the cycle with the coefficient vector. For each distinct cycle length L we (1) compute all binomial coefficients C(K,t) using factorials, (2) build coeff[0…L‑1] by adding C(K,t) to coeff[t%L] for t=0…K, (3) for every cycle of that length compute the circular convolution via a Number Theoretic Transform (NTT) modulo 998244353 (a prime suitable for NTT). The convolution of two length‑L vectors a and b is obtained by a normal convolution of length 2L and then adding the tail part to the head part to obtain the circular result. All cycles together contain exactly N elements, therefore the total work of all NTTs is O(N log N). The overall complexity is O(N log N + K + Σ distinct L·log L) which fits easily into the limits. Edge cases such as K=0 (coeff[0]=1, others 0) or cycles of length 1 are handled automatically.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N + K + D·L_max log L_max) where D is the number of distinct cycle lengths (D ≤ √N). In practice O(N log N + K).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N + K) for storing the array, factorials, and temporary NTT buffers.
